narrative:
I'm sorry to share this news, but the law school's admission model predicted a 60% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which led to the rejection of your application. This prediction is based on your academic record, test scores, family background, and other personal details compared to thousands of past students. You ranked in the lowest decile for your school's grades in both Year 1 and Year 3, far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack performance around the 5th or 6th decile. Your LSAT score of 24 was also notably lower than the average of about 37, and your undergraduate GPA of 3.0 sat just under the usual 3.2. Coming from a lower-middle family income bracket, being Asian (a smaller group in the dataset at under 6%, versus over 80% White students), and planning full-time work were additional factors, though your gender as male aligned more closely with the majority.

In assessing your profile, the model weighed these elements, with your Year 3 decile rank having the strongest influence, pushing the prediction toward failure more than any other factor. This was followed closely by your LSAT score, then your Year 1 decile, family income, racial background, undergraduate GPA, gender, and finally your full-time status, each contributing in descending order to the overall risk assessment—all nudging the outcome toward a higher chance of not passing the bar. Compared to typical students who often succeed, your consistently bottom-decile school rankings and below-average LSAT stood out as bigger drags than your GPA or background details. While no single factor dooms an application, these combined to tip the scales against you in a way that stronger academic showings might have offset.

Looking ahead, you can't change your past grades, LSAT, GPA, family income, race, or gender, but you could consider switching to part-time work if feasible, as full-time commitments were a minor factor here. More importantly, focus on bolstering your bar exam preparation through targeted study habits or prep courses, drawing from the lesson that early academic ranks like yours signal a need for stronger foundational skills. Retaking the LSAT for a higher score could help future applications if the school allows updates. With dedication, many students overcome similar profiles—keep building your strengths, and you'll have a clearer path forward.
